selector.exact: Is there a logic of history?

annotations.anchor: Is there a logic of history?
annotations.note: Spengler opens the whole work with this question. For a first reading, treat the Introduction as the thesis in miniature: everything that follows is an attempt to answer yes — that history has a discernible form, a 'morphology', as exact as the form of a plant. Don't get lost in the cascade of examples; hold onto the question.

Spengler's boast that the future stages of a Culture can be foretold is the single claim later scholars found least defensible. According to H. Stuart Hughes, Spengler is most valuable read not as a prophet whose dating can be checked, but as a morphologist of cultural style — the predictive apparatus is the part of the system that has worn worst.

'Morphology' is the study of living forms and their transformations — a method Spengler takes directly from Goethe's botany and applies to history. Cultures are treated not as a chain of causes but as organisms with a shape and a life-cycle. This is the master-method of the entire book.

selector.exact: The Civilization is the inevitable destiny of the Culture

annotations.anchor: Civilization is the inevitable destiny of the Culture
annotations.note: The book's central opposition. A 'Culture' (Kultur) is the living, creative spring-and-summer of a people's soul; 'Civilization' (Zivilisation) is its frozen winter — the late, megalopolitan, irreligious, money-and-machine stage after creative force is spent. Spengler insists the West has already crossed from the one into the other; the 'decline' is this crossing.

selector.exact: a creation of the Magian world-sense

annotations.note: 'Magian' is Spengler's name for the Culture of the early Christian, Jewish, Byzantine and Islamic East — the soul whose prime-symbol is the world as a cavern, an enclosed space lit between heaven and earth. He treats Christianity, the Talmud, and the Qur'an as expressions of one and the same Magian springtime.

selector.exact: “contemporary” in our special sense—with the phase of Hellenism

annotations.note: Spengler's idea of 'contemporaneity': two events in different Cultures are 'contemporary' when they occupy the same position in their respective life-cycles, regardless of calendar date. Our own modern age, he argues, stands where Hellenism stood in the Classical — both are the Culture's late, sceptical, cosmopolitan autumn. This homology is the engine of the Comparative Tables.

selector.exact: the Copernican discovery in the historical sphere

annotations.anchor: the Copernican discovery in the historical sphere
annotations.note: A characteristic Spenglerian gesture: just as Copernicus displaced the Earth from the centre of the cosmos, Spengler claims to displace Western Europe from the centre of history, demoting 'ancient–medieval–modern' to a parochial, Ptolemaic conceit. Watch how often he reaches for an astronomical or mathematical figure to dignify a historical claim.

selector.exact: conceptions of decadence, militarism, the transvaluation of all

annotations.anchor: decadence … the transvaluation of all values
annotations.note: Spengler names Nietzsche as one of his two masters ('Goethe gave me method, Nietzsche the questioning faculty'). 'Decadence' and 'the transvaluation of all values' are Nietzsche's terms; Spengler borrows the diagnosis of cultural exhaustion while discarding Nietzsche's hope of an overcoming. The two share a vocabulary and a mood, not a remedy.

'Decline' renders the German Untergang — literally a 'going-under', the setting of the sun, a downfall. Atkinson's 'Decline' is gentler than the German; Spengler's title Der Untergang des Abendlandes carries the finality of a sunset (Abendland, the 'evening-land', is itself the German word for the West). The title is an image, not a forecast of catastrophe.

selector.exact: we have the perfectly ahistoric soul

annotations.note: Spengler ranks Cultures by their relation to time, calling the Classical and Indian souls 'ahistoric' — incapable, he claims, of feeling history as the West does. Read this as a value-laden hierarchy typical of early-20th-century German thought: it is doing analytic work for his system, but it also flattens Indian and Greek thought into a foil for the 'Faustian'. Later scholarship treats the ranking as the system's bias, not its discovery.
